author: @howardsimpson489
text: The first quartz crystal oscillators were made during WW1. By the 1920s, these were very stable and temperature insensitive. Methods of subdividing the high frequencies were developed pre WW2 so very accurate clocks were possible. It is no wonder escapement timekeeping became niche marketing like vinyl records. Atomic clock accuracy is now seconds in millions of years but they are currently too big for your pocket. Who knows, in a few years this problem may go away. Thanks for your demo unit Robert.

author: @JD_Mortal
text: You can make stacked springs to get a better travel. Similar to stacking planetary gear boxes. A plate between springs, on the axle. The outside of one spring attached to the plate and the inner of the second spring attached to the plate. Combined, its like having a spring twice as large, in a smaller space. Also, you naturally get more flex, due to the split load. Just make sure the disk that joins them, has a wide spread across the axle. It will want to tilt between the two opposing forces attachments.

author: @JD_Mortal
text:
Is there any way to contact you directly? I am working on a show series, where the progression of the series is aided by the progression of a clock. First made simple, with minimal tools, but it ultimately ends up becoming a complex structure. However, I am having difficulty capturing two components of the initial "simple" design. The initial design uses peg-gears. The first issue is the power drive gear. I want two levers that virtually extend the gear, for a greater initial gearing ratio and precision. The extensions flip-flop, between the large gears pegs, yielding ten additional pegs at the end of each extension. One would drive power, while the other is pushed off the pegs and gravity drops the free extension. The free extension lands below the powering extension, extending the drive by another ten pegs. Repeat once the top drive extension becomes free, and it falls to extend it again. The purpose of this action is to reduce the need for a super massive power gear, which has to power a smaller gear, with a similar setup, to get the final output power.

The second issue I have, is a stepped series of 7 weights. 6 are used to actually drive the pendulum and control timing. Not the massive gear setup, mentioned above. They are going to provide ten minutes of power each, except the seventh weight. 1-6 power the pendulum, the 7th is only used to lift weight 6, after 6 falls. While weight 6 is falling, the power, from the above mentioned setup, will lift weights 1-5 and 7. When weight 6 ends, at the bottom, that triggers weight 1 to start falling, and then triggers weight 7 to release. Weight 7 raises weight 6, so it is ready to be used for power after weight 2, 3, 4 and 5 have each fallen, in series. Repeat...

Any abundance of energy, from lifting the 6 weights, and the weights reaching the bottom and energy harvested from a pendulum overcharge, is captured and sent to a similar release mechanism as weight 7. That stored energy is put back into lifting the primary drive weight, when there is enough to crank it to one of the ratchet winding positions. (Not perpetual motion, the harvested energy is low and it takes many cycles to get enough energy to crank one ratchet position. The intention is to allow a better solid delivery of power, for lifting, without excessive unwinding losses.)

The power for the main drive, will be a 400 pound weight. The weight will run through a pulley block of 4x X2, for a ratio of 1:8 or 8:1, depending on perception. The result is a drive wire that can offer 50 lbs of drive at 8 meters of cable. The weight only drops 1 meter, each winding.

That's the "simple" first clock. 🤣 The following clocks will eventually look like normal clocks, but gain additional dials for various things. Power charging will be a combination of wind, stream flooding, and walking platform power harvesting. (So it never needs manual winding. Eventually they get real power for a real clock, but it always remains mechanical. Like a monument "Big Ben", but it retains alt power to reduce consumed electricity, as it gets more complex, in time.)

text: I seem to recall Marshall from Wristwatch Revival talking about position error and saying that it's mostly down to friction in the balance staff bearings. That's why watchmakers regulate watches by checking the accuracy in several different positions.

author: @Woffy.
text:
Pendulums are sometimes Ok,  My 244 year old Grandfather clock (Bill) yesterday was two seconds fast after seven days, the temperature this week as been stable at 17.5 C and the pendulum is happy. It will slow a few seconds a week as the weather improves and catch up in the winter. The mechanism is very worn and sloppy but does its job.    
Nice model Mr Smith.

author: @mfree80286
text: I've had great luck with using larger bearings for light-force items by peeling the shields off and flushing the grease out of the cage, then giving it just a little graphite. I've got a 6203ZZ in front of me right now that's had that treatment and the outer race can be spun like a fidget spinner for a 3-4 second rundown. Will it last? No. Will it take the rated load? No. Will it let you use an easier to manage/easier to obtain bearing type in a printed application where free running is more valuable than load capacity? Absolutely.

author: @KronosGodwisen
text:
We're getting a bit steam punk. Or is it wind punk? Looking cool.

Is it better to stick to the same plastic for that one pin that is experiencing stress or use a stronger material? Plastic might snap. Metal might damage the other parts. Compromise with wood, or is that even more brittle?

It's getting more complicated, and cooler, with the clockwork. It's sure to be a great way to get power at a steady rate.

author: @morphles
text: Cool and all, but plastic springs are super duper lame, as they loose too much energy. Got that problem with my lego clock experiments. So I strongly suggest using somewhat more real spring :)

replies.author: @l0I0I0I0
replies.text: Just a prototype.

replies.author: @morphles
replies.text: @l0I0I0I0 Doesn't matter, the scale of difference is just way too large. If you try to proceed you'll soon see that input is wildly different to plastic vs plastic spring. Unless you mean it's toy/proof of concept :) Seriously, the difference is very significant.

replies.author: @l0I0I0I0
replies.text: ​@morphles  ty for the heads up. Yes modifications are expected and the spring  will be vastly different amd diff material. But thanks for the note!

replies.author: @MostlyPennyCat
replies.text:
​@l0I0I0I0
You can still build a prototype out of inappropriate materials and call it a prototype.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: To be honest - it is always a question of what to do - i get what you are saying but you have to remember i have a different objective than just producing a model to copy. The main idea here is as a kind of teaching aid. I am more interested in folks getting hold of what it is, how it's put together and what aspects are important. That way I hope they will take those ideas and design their own mechanisms rather than copy mine. It's why I left out the regulator. The idea is folks will want to design their own regulator and hopefully improve this with better materials and more complex design as the basic idea has been explored - or at least that is my hope. So, I like your comment - you are quite right - but I also hope you can see why I did a plastic spring.
